tipRules.tip: Hackney: 'Fish want a big, slow-moving bait in the pre-prespawn. A bladed jig keeps the bait in the strike zone longer because males are roaming a big area and are really aggressive.'
Hackney: 'When fish first come up shallow to set up for spawn, a swim jig with a Rage Craw trailer keeps the bait in the strike area longer because the males are aggressive and roaming.'

Hackney: 'At the tail end of spawn when males guard fry, the swim jig comes back into play. Bass are no longer on the bottom guarding beds - focus on the upper water column. Drag a swim jig through fry areas and the male will attack it.'
Hackney: 'A Red Eye Shad (lipless crankbait) is one of my go-to pre-prespawn baits when fish are aggressive coming out of winter.'
Hackney: 'A spinnerbait is one of my key baits for the pre-prespawn phase when fish are aggressive and setting up shallow.'

Hackney: 'When females are done spawning, reach for the flipping jig. Females bunch up after spawning - when you find one, you find a group. In Louisiana, there will be a female on every cypress tree in the area.'

knowledge.insight: Hackney: 'There are two phases for prespawn: pre-prespawn right out of winter and then prespawn. These two phases are your best bet at catching a personal best. Fish are stressed from spawning prep - males guard a huge territory and are really aggressive.'

Hackney: 'In the early spawn, nothing beats a piece of plastic. Use baits without a lot of action like a tube or Rodent - the only action is from your rod tip. Be patient with repetitive casts to bedding bass.'
Hackney: 'Once water hits the 70s, fish pay more attention to baits with appendages that swim. Use something like a Game Hawg or Rage Bug. Fish grab it even when reeling back in.'
Hackney: 'Especially in the South, females like to bunch up after spawning. When you find one female, you find a group of them. Use topwater around those groups as females seem to like it more.'
